# Rusdantic

> Pydantic for Rust — a high-ergonomics data validation and serialization framework. One derive macro generates Serialize, Deserialize, and Validate. Validate-on-deserialize ensures invalid structs never exist in memory.

Rusdantic unifies serde serialization, deserialization, and validation into a single `#[derive(Rusdantic)]` macro for Rust. Inspired by Python's Pydantic, it provides path-aware errors, type coercion, field aliases, JSON Schema generation, PII redaction, sanitizers, partial validation, constrained types, and settings management — all zero-cost at compile time.

## Install

```toml
[dependencies]
rusdantic = "0.1.0"
```

## Quick Start

```rust
use rusdantic::prelude::*;

#[derive(Rusdantic, Debug)]
struct User {
    #[rusdantic(length(min = 3, max = 20))]
    username: String,

    #[rusdantic(email)]
    email: String,

    #[rusdantic(range(min = 18))]
    age: u8,
}

fn main() {
    let json = r#"{"username": "rust_ace", "email": "user@example.com", "age": 25}"#;
    let user: User = rusdantic::from_json(json).unwrap();
}
```

## Features

- One Derive, Three Traits: #[derive(Rusdantic)] generates Serialize, Deserialize, Validate
- Validate-on-Deserialize: invalid structs never exist in memory
- Path-Aware Errors: precise error paths like user.addresses[0].zip_code
- 7 Built-in Validators: length, range, email, url, pattern, contains, required
- Custom Validators: field-level and struct-level cross-field validation
- Type Coercion: lax mode accepts "123" as integer, "true" as bool
- Field Aliases: separate names for deserialization, serialization, and error paths
- Enum Support: validates fields inside enum variants
- JSON Schema: Draft 2020-12 / OpenAPI 3.1 schema generation
- PII Redaction: #[rusdantic(redact)] hides sensitive data with constant-time comparison
- Sanitizers: trim, lowercase, uppercase, truncate during deserialization
- Partial Validation: validate field subsets for PATCH endpoints
- DumpOptions: include/exclude fields, exclude_none, pretty-print
- Constrained Types: PositiveInt, EmailStr, SecretStr, HttpUrl, FiniteFloat, NonEmptyString
- Settings Management: load config from env vars, .env files, JSON
- Zero-Cost: validation logic monomorphized at compile time

## Feature Comparison vs Alternatives

| Feature | serde + validator | serde + garde | Rusdantic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Setup | 3+ crates | 2+ crates | 1 crate |
| Validate on deser | No | No | Yes |
| Type coercion | serde_with per-field | No | Configurable |
| Field aliases | serde(alias) only | No | 3 alias types |
| Sanitizers | No | No | Built-in |
| JSON Schema | Separate (schemars) | No | Built-in |
| PII Redaction | No | No | 3 modes |
| Partial validation | No | No | Built-in |
| Secret types | No | No | SecretStr |
| Settings mgmt | No | No | Built-in |

## Crate Ecosystem

- rusdantic: Facade crate (re-exports everything)
- rusdantic-core: Runtime traits, errors, validators, coercion, dump
- rusdantic-derive: Proc macro #[derive(Rusdantic)]
- rusdantic-types: Constrained types (PositiveInt, EmailStr, SecretStr, HttpUrl)
- rusdantic-settings: Settings management (env, dotenv, JSON)

## Documentation

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/mmgehlot/rusdantic)
- [Website](https://mmgehlot.github.io/rusdantic/)
- [crates.io](https://crates.io/crates/rusdantic)
- [docs.rs](https://docs.rs/rusdantic)

## When to Use Rusdantic

Use Rusdantic when you need data validation in Rust and want:
- A single derive macro instead of juggling serde + validator + schemars
- Invalid data rejected during deserialization (not after)
- Path-aware error messages for API responses
- Type coercion for flexible input parsing
- JSON Schema generation from your types
- PII-safe logging with redacted Debug output
- PATCH endpoint support with partial validation
